formations_blocks: Brazil circulating possession from a back four
Haiti settled into a compact defensive shape / low block
adjustments: Vinicius drifting infield from the left to start moves
Casemiro dropping deep to control tempo
Raphinha providing the main running-in-behind threat on the right
Brazil forced to recycle across the back four when forward options were blocked
key_matchups: Raphinha vs Haiti’s back line on the right channel
Douglas vs Haiti’s challenge leading to Arcus’s yellow card
phases.summary: Brazil started slowly and lacked clear verticality, with many players coming deep to receive and only Raphinha consistently looking to run behind. Haiti were organised, comfortable, and quick to clear or break when possible, keeping Brazil at arm’s length despite the early pressure. The phase hinted that Brazil’s best route would be direct balls into the space behind Haiti’s static defence rather than patient circulation.

formations_blocks: Brazil laboured in possession and passive in defence
Haiti defended compactly before trying to counter
Brazil attacking through corners and over-the-top passes
adjustments: Vinicius Jr cut in from the left
Casemiro repeatedly looked for Raphinha over the top
Haiti briefly threatened down the right on transition
key_matchups: Casemiro vs Haiti's back line with balls over the top to Raphinha
Raphinha vs Placide on the one-on-one chance
Haiti's right-sided overlap attempt vs Brazil's offside line
phases.summary: Brazil began this phase flat in possession and passive without the ball, while Haiti looked to break quickly down the right when space opened. The deadlock was eventually broken by Brazil’s individual quality: Vinicius Jr drove inside to force a save, and Cunha finished the resulting rebound scramble for 1-0 before scoring again later in the half.

formations_blocks: Brazil in a control-oriented attacking setup
Haiti in a defensive setup that limited them to zero xG
adjustments: Two Haiti substitutions at the break: Arcus off for Simon, Pierrot off for Isidor
Brazil described as effective in transition and in behind through Matheus Cunha
Casemiro breaking up play and Guimarães advancing the ball from midfield
Paquetá providing direct, over-the-top moments of quality
phases.summary: Brazil go into half-time 3-0 up, but the performance is described as functional rather than sparkling. The tactical theme is Brazil’s midfield control and vertical threat through Cunha and Paquetá, alongside Casemiro’s ball-winning and Guimarães’ progression, while Haiti offered little attacking threat and registered zero xG. Haiti’s two changes at the break suggest a search for a response after being largely contained.

adjustments: Haiti switched from a setup with Simon at the base of midfield to a back four
Haiti moved into a 4-4-2 to apply extra pressure on Brazil's midfield
Brazil became notably passive out of possession, allowing Haiti more of the ball
phases.summary: The key tactical change after the break was Haiti’s shift into a 4-4-2, with Simon anchoring from midfield and the aim of pressing Brazil’s midfield more aggressively than in the first half. That brought more territory and possession for Haiti, but Brazil’s passive 4-4-2 out of possession and occasional stepping out to intercept still prevented clear chances, while Brazil remained dangerous in transition and through Vinicius on the left.

formations_blocks: Brazil in a compact defensive shape, protecting their box and clearing danger quickly
Haiti circulating possession around Brazil’s box but struggling to break through a settled defensive block
Brazil transitioning into a methodical counterattacking shape when they win it back
adjustments: Brazil make late substitutions: Ederson and Danilo Santos on for Vinicius and Guimaraes
Brazil adopt a more conservative late-game approach after the subs, with less attacking intent
key_matchups: Rayan vs Haiti’s pressure in midfield/transition
Isidor vs Alisson at the near post
Deedson vs Brazil’s marking on the left
phases.summary: Brazil spend this phase managing the game, defending their own danger zone smartly and using the occasional counterattack to threaten, while Haiti repeatedly get the ball into useful wide areas without finding penetration. Haiti’s best moments come from long balls and left-sided entries, but Brazil’s compact defending, plus Alisson’s smart stop at the near post, keep them at arm’s length as the match winds down.
